summary: A Central Asian ethnic group of nomadic horsemen unified under Genghis Khan in 1206, who built the largest contiguous land empire in world history, stretching from Korea to Eastern Europe. Clues most often identify the Mongols through their secret law code, the Yassa; their tribal-council system of election, the kurultai; and their own chronicle, the Secret History of the Mongols, which recounts Genghis Khan's rise from the kidnapping of his wife Borte to his unification of the steppe. After Genghis Khan's death the empire split among his descendants into the Yuan Dynasty in China, the Golden Horde in Russia, and the Ilkhanate in Persia, while a remnant Oirat group, the Dzungars, survived into the 18th century before being destroyed by the Qing. Modern Mongolia emerged from Qing rule in 1911 and, after decades as a Soviet-aligned socialist state, held its Democratic Revolution in 1990.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: A nomadic, equestrian ethnic group of the Central Asian steppe, united under Genghis Khan (born Temujin) in 1206 to found the Mongol Empire, the largest contiguous land empire in world history. Giveaway clues most often identify the Mongols through their secret law code, the Yassa, which was largely tolerant of the religions of conquered peoples; their tribal assemblies called kurultai, which elected new khans; and their own chronicle, the Secret History of the Mongols. They practiced sky worship centered on the god Tengri alongside later Tibetan Buddhist influence, and produced formidable warrior women such as Khutulun, a wrestling princess who advised her father Kaidu.

clues.clue: United by Genghis Khan (born Temujin) in 1206 into the largest contiguous land empire in world history
examples: name this Central Asian ethnic group united by Genghis Khan.
name this people who ruled the largest contiguous empire in world history thanks to Genghis Khan.

clues.clue: Kept a secret, largely religiously-tolerant law code called the Yassa
examples: It is believed that these people's near-total religious toleration was once enshrined in a secret law code called the Yassa.
These people elected leaders through councils called kurultai and kept the Yassa law code.

clues.clue: New leaders were elected at tribal council assemblies called kurultai, at which a leader was seated on white felt and given a golden sword
examples: Leaders of these people were chosen by being seated on white felt and given a golden sword in a ceremony.
Those elections, the kurultai, were described in a Secret History of this group.

clues.clue: Kaidu's daughter, the wrestling princess Khutulun, defeated thousands of suitors in wrestling matches and used a tactic of grabbing an enemy soldier and dragging him back to her father
examples: Another princess of this people defeated thousands of suitors in wrestling matches and advised her father Kaidu.
A commander of these people used the tactic of darting out from the lines, grabbing one enemy soldier, and dragging him back to her father; that woman was Khutulun.

clues.clue: Divided clans into "black bone" relatives, among whom marriage was allowed, and "white bone" family, among whom it was not
examples: In this people's clan structure, marriage was allowed amongst "black bone" relatives but not "white bone" family.

clues.clue: Two men could swear a bond of ritual brotherhood called an anda, sealed with an exchange of gifts such as a black sable coat
examples: Two men belonging to this ethnic group could make a pledge of brotherhood called an anda.
This culture's "anda" oath created a fictitious family relationship between people.

clues.clue: Practiced sky worship centered on the god Tengri, though Islam later spread in lands they conquered from the Khwarazmian Empire
examples: Although these people practiced sky worship, Islam spread in the lands they conquered from the Khwarazmian Empire.

works.name: Genghis Khan and the Unification of the Mongols
works.description: Born Temujin and, according to legend, clutching a blood clot in his fist, Genghis Khan unified the fractious Mongol tribes into a single empire by 1206. His rise is recounted in the Secret History of the Mongols: his wife Borte was kidnapped by the rival Merkit tribe and rescued by Temujin and his sworn blood-brother (anda) Jamukha, with whom he later broke after subduing the Naimans and Merkits, inflicting on Jamukha a bloodless death by strangulation. Temujin's rule brought previously warring clans an organizing structure -- the elite bodyguard called the kheshig, army units called tumen, and the countrywide yam relay-messenger system -- while his mother-in-law's people, the Khereids, and other tribes were absorbed into the growing Mongol state.

clues.clue: Born with the name Temujin, allegedly clutching a blood clot in his fist at birth
examples: This group was founded by a man with the birth name Temujin.
One of these people was allegedly born with a blood clot in his fist, as recorded in these people's Secret History; that man was Temujin.

clues.clue: His wife Borte was kidnapped by the Merkit tribe and rescued by Temujin and his blood-brother (anda) Jamukha
examples: This ethnic group's Secret History discusses how Borte gave birth shortly after being kidnapped and rescued by her husband, Temujin.
The leader who unified these people had a wife who was kidnapped by a group of them, the Merkits.

clues.clue: Broke with his sworn blood-brother Jamukha after subduing the Naimans and Merkits, inflicting a "death without blood" on him
examples: A "Secret History" recounts how a leader of these people inflicted "death without blood" on his blood-brother Jamukha after subduing the Naimans and Merkits.
The Tibetans share the myth of King Gesar with these people, whose ruler rose against his blood brother Jamukha as told in their Secret History.

clues.clue: Created the elite bodyguard called the kheshig and organized the army into units of 10,000 called tumen
examples: The kheshig was the most elite group within this organization, which was divided into units of 10,000 called tumen.
A character in this text creates a bodyguard called the kheshig led by the sons of his regiment commanders.

clues.clue: Established the yam relay-messenger system, using seal-passes called paiza
examples: This organization communicated orders over long distances via the yam system.
The paiza was a pass that certified that a holder of this title had granted the possessor tax exemption.

clues.clue: Had Tata-Tonga, a captured member of the Naiman tribe, create a writing script for the Mongols
examples: A character in this text...also commands Tata-Tonga to create a writing script.

works.name: The Secret History of the Mongols
works.description: A 13th-century Mongolian-language chronicle of Genghis Khan's life and lineage, discovered in the 19th century by the Russian Archimandrite Palladius (who produced a partial translation) and shares its name with a text by the Byzantine historian Procopius. It opens with a mythic genealogy in which the Mongols' ancestor Alan Gua is impregnated by a "glittering spirit," and traces her descendants back to a blue-gray wolf and a red doe. It narrates Temujin's exchange of knucklebones with Jamukha to form an anda pact, the kidnapping and rescue of Borte, and the creation of the kheshig, ending in the reign of Ogedei Khan.

clues.clue: Discovered in the 19th century by the Russian Archimandrite Palladius, who produced a partial English/Russian translation
examples: This text was discovered in the 19th century by the Archimandrite Palladius.
This man compiled a partial English translation of the Secret History. He also produced a six-volume translation of The Tale of Genji.

clues.clue: Opens with a genealogy in which the ancestress Alan Gua is impregnated by a "glittering spirit," descending from a blue-gray wolf and a red doe
examples: This text describes a ray of light that impregnates Alan the Fair in a genealogy passage that begins with the marriage of a doe and a wolf.
According to legend, the first of these people was brought about by a blue-gray wolf and a red doe.

clues.clue: Shares its title with a work by the Byzantine historian Procopius
examples: name this history of Genghis Khan that shares its name with a text by Procopius.

clues.clue: Ends its narrative in the reign of Ogedei Khan
examples: This text's later chapters describe the fall of the Khwarezmian Kingdom, and it ends in the reign of Ogedei.
Identify this work, which concludes with a description of the reign of Ogedei.

works.name: The Mongol Conquest of Jin and Song China
works.description: The Mongols' decades-long conquest of China began under Genghis Khan with an attack through the Badger's Mouth pass that devastated the Jurchen-ruled Jin dynasty; Ogedei Khan oversaw the Jin's final fall, while the Toluid Civil War between Kublai Khan and Ariq Boke delayed the campaign against the Southern Song for three years. Fighting deployed counterweight trebuchets nicknamed "Muslim Paos" and gunpowder-based "thunder-crash bombs," and the Song general Wen Tianxiang's resistance ultimately failed when the Song navy, despite a ten-to-one numerical advantage, was destroyed at the 1279 Battle of Yamen, after which Kublai Khan proclaimed the Yuan Dynasty.

clues.clue: Opened with a slaughter of Jin troops at the Badger's Mouth pass
examples: In the early stages of this campaign, a large number of troops were slaughtered at the Badger's Mouth pass.

clues.clue: Combatants used counterweight trebuchets called "Muslim Paos" and gunpowder "thunder-crash bombs"
examples: Opposing sides in this campaign deployed counterweight trebuchets called "Muslim Paos" and used iron chains to lower devastating "thunder-crash bombs".

clues.clue: The Toluid Civil War (between Kublai Khan and his brother Ariq Boke) delayed the campaign against the Song for three years
examples: The Toluid civil war derailed this campaign for three years.

clues.clue: Ogedei Khan oversaw the final fall of the Jin dynasty
examples: The early stage of this campaign ended with Ogedei overseeing the fall of the Jin dynasty.

clues.clue: The Song navy, despite a ten-to-one numerical advantage, was destroyed at the 1279 Battle of Yamen, ending the campaign
examples: This campaign as a whole ended after a navy overcame a ten to one numerical disadvantage at the Battle of Yamen.
The Battle of Yaishan successfully concluded this campaign in 1279.

works.name: The Yuan Dynasty
works.description: The Chinese dynasty founded by Kublai Khan after the 1279 Battle of Yamen, ruling from capitals at Shangdu ("Xanadu") and Khanbaliq. It issued paper currency called chao made from mulberry bark, commissioned the 'Phags-pa script as a universal writing system, and saw the rise of the zaju theatrical genre, including Guan Hanqing's The Injustice to Dou E. Foreign visitors included Marco Polo, whose travels were recorded by Rustichello da Pisa, and the Franciscan friar Odoric of Pordenone; court dietitian Hu Sihui published an early treatise linking malnutrition and disease. The dynasty declined after the corrupt finance minister Ahmad Fanakati's tenure and the Ispah rebellion at the port of Zaytun, and was overthrown in 1368 by the Red Turban Rebellion, led by the future Hongwu Emperor Zhu Yuanzhang, whose White Lotus-linked rebels wore distinctive red headwear.

clues.clue: Founded by Kublai Khan, ruling from capitals at Shangdu ("Xanadu") and Khanbaliq
examples: name this Chinese dynasty that preceded the Ming and was once ruled by Kublai Khan.
Accounts of this dynasty's capitals at Shangdu and Khanbaliq were written by Rustichello da Pisa on behalf of a Venetian merchant.

clues.clue: Issued paper currency called chao, made from mulberry-tree bark
examples: The bark of mulberry trees was used to create this dynasty's chao currency.

clues.clue: Commissioned the 'Phags-pa script as a universal writing system for Chinese and Tibetan
examples: This dynasty published the 'Phags-Pa as a universal script for the Chinese and Tibetan languages.
Rulers of this ethnic group commissioned a Tibetan monk to create the Phags-pa alphabet.

clues.clue: Hosted zaju theater, including Guan Hanqing's play The Injustice to Dou E
examples: This dynasty's theatergoers enjoyed zaju performances of works such as Guan Hanqing's The Injustice to Dou E.

clues.clue: Was visited by Marco Polo, whose travels were recorded by Rustichello da Pisa, and by the friar Odoric of Pordenone
examples: This dynasty hosted explorers such as Ibn Battuta and Marco Polo.
During the rule of this dynasty, Odoric of Pordenone recounted his stay in a monastery in Zaiton.

clues.clue: Employed the corrupt finance minister Ahmad Fanakati, and saw the Ispah rebellion at the port of Zaytun
examples: This polity employed the corrupt finance minister Ahmad Fanakati.
The Ispah rebellion occurred in this polity's port of Zaytun, which is the origin of the English word "satin."

clues.clue: Overthrown in 1368 by the Red Turban Rebellion, led by the future Hongwu Emperor Zhu Yuanzhang
examples: This dynasty consolidated its power after the Battle of Yamen, and it was overthrown by the Hongwu Emperor in the Red Turban Rebellion.
Name this rebellion, which was led by the man who would become the Hongwu Emperor, Zhu Yuanzhang. It took its name from its participants' trademark headwear.

clues.clue: A Northern Yuan remnant survived in Mongolia and was restored in the 15th century under Queen Mandukhai the Wise and Dayan Khan
examples: In the 15th century, the "Northern" version of this dynasty experienced a restoration under Queen Mandukhai the Wise and Dayan Khan.

works.name: The Mongol Invasions of Japan
works.description: Kublai Khan launched two failed invasions of Japan, in 1274 (the Bun'ei campaign) and 1281 (the Koan campaign), both beginning with battles on Tsushima Island and both ultimately destroyed by typhoons the Japanese called kamikaze, or "divine wind." A key primary source is a scroll commissioned by the warrior Takezaki Suenaga to glorify his own disobedient bravery at the Battle of Bun'ei, and a leader of the So clan died defending Tsushima. The invasions spurred Japan's Soshu school of swordsmithing, which studied blades broken in the fighting, and left the Kamakura shogunate unable to reward its gokenin vassals with land, weakening the shogunate and elevating rival feudal landlords.

clues.clue: Both invasions (1274 and 1281) began with battles on Tsushima Island
examples: Both of these campaigns began with battles fought on Tsushima Island.

clues.clue: Destroyed by typhoons the Japanese called kamikaze, or "divine wind"
examples: Large parts of the invading forces in these campaigns were wiped out by typhoons named kamikaze.
name this 'divine wind' that twice destroyed Mongol invasion fleets aimed at Japan.

clues.clue: A scroll commissioned by the warrior Takezaki Suenaga, who disobeyed orders to be first into battle, is a key primary source
examples: A major historical source from these campaigns is a scroll commissioned by a single warrior to glorify his actions at the Battle of Bun'ei, where he disobeyed his superiors to be the first in battle.

clues.clue: Inspired the Soshu school of Japanese swordsmithing, which studied blades broken in the fighting
examples: The Soshu school of swordsmithing emerged by studying blades broken during these campaigns.

clues.clue: Left the Kamakura shogunate unable to reward its gokenin vassals with conquered land, weakening it and elevating rival feudal landlords
examples: Due to the failed invasions, the Kamakura shogunate had no land to provide as rewards to the gokenin, whose significance was supplanted by these feudal landlords.

works.name: The Mongol Invasion of Europe
works.description: Batu Khan led a 1241 invasion of Eastern Europe on the pretext of pursuing the Cuman leader Koten, with his general Subotai as the campaign's strategic mind. At the Battle of Legnica, Mongol forces killed Duke Henry the Pious of Silesia and paraded his head on a spike, filling nine sacks with the right ears of slain Polish knights (an event still commemorated by Krakow's Lajkonik festival); legend credits the monk Czeslaw with saving Breslau from the invasion with a summoned fireball. Days later, at the Battle of Mohi (fought near the Sajo river), Subotai lured Hungarian King Bela IV's forces -- weakened after Bela's expulsion of the Cumans -- into a marsh and annihilated them, opening Hungary to devastation. The campaign ended only when the 1241 death of Great Khan Ogedei forced Batu and Subotai to return east for a kurultai to elect his successor.

clues.clue: Launched by Batu Khan in 1241 on the pretext of pursuing the Cuman leader Koten, who had taken refuge with Hungary's King Bela IV
examples: The pretext for this military campaign was the refuge given to Koten, leader of the Cumans.
name this 1241 campaign in which Batu Khan invaded the continent west of Asia.

clues.clue: At the Battle of Legnica, Duke Henry the Pious of Silesia was killed and his head paraded on a spike
examples: One battle during this campaign saw the death of Duke Henry the Pious.

According to legend, a trumpeter at Saint Mary's Church was killed while warning against an invasion by these people that ended with the head of Duke Henry the Pious being paraded on a spike at the Battle of Legnica.

clues.clue: Nine sacks were filled with the right ears of slain Polish knights after Legnica, an event still commemorated by Krakow's Lajkonik festival
examples: The defeat of this group is commemorated by the Lajkonik festival, which remembers a war in which this group counted their kills by filling nine sacks with right ears.
Nine sacks were filled with the right ears of many Polish knights during this campaign's Battle of Liegnitz.

clues.clue: At the Battle of Mohi (near the Sajo river), Subotai lured Hungarian forces -- weakened after Bela IV expelled the Cumans -- into a marshy trap and annihilated them
examples: Subotai used this specific tactic to trick Hungarian knights into falling into a marshy death-trap at the Battle of Mohi.
Before this campaign's decisive Battle of Sajo, King Bela IV made the disastrous decision to expel the Cuman people from his realm.

clues.clue: Ended when the 1241 death of Great Khan Ogedei forced a return east for a kurultai to choose his successor
examples: The death of a leader of this group caused their general Subotai to retreat from Vienna in order to elect a new one.
This campaign was not completed due to the death of Ogedei Khan, though the strategic mind behind the main course of this campaign was Subotai.

works.name: The Golden Horde
works.description: The northwestern successor khanate founded by Batu Khan, grandson of Genghis Khan, whose name supposedly derives from the color of its shining tents (it is also called the Kipchak Khanate or Ulus of Jochi). Ruling from its capital at Sarai, it imposed a tributary "yoke" over the Rus principalities, issuing patents of authority called yarlyks -- Alexander Nevsky continued to pay tribute even after defeating the Swedes and Teutonic Knights. Its power began to wane after Dmitri Donskoi's Russian victory at the 1380 Battle of Kulikovo (commemorated in the epic Zadonshchina), and it was decisively broken when Ivan III forced its retreat at the 1480 Great Stand on the Ugra River. Centralized by Tokhtamysh's unification of its "Blue" and "White" branches, the Horde's institutions -- including a fivefold army division of Sentry, Left Hand, and Right Hand regiments -- shaped Russian statecraft even as it declined.

clues.clue: Founded by Batu Khan, grandson of Genghis Khan; its name supposedly derives from the color of its shining tents
examples: name this Mongol successor state founded by Batu Khan and supposedly named for its colorful tents.
Name this empire, which was founded by Batu Khan, the grandson of Genghis Khan.

clues.clue: Ruled from its capital at Sarai, issuing patents of authority called yarlyks over Russian princes
examples: the Battle of Kulikovo was a defeat for what polity, whose khans ruled from Sarai?
While in this state's capital to obtain a patent, or yarlyk, St. Michael was executed for refusing to walk between two fires.

clues.clue: Alexander Nevsky continued to pay tribute to it even after defeating the Swedes at the Neva and the Teutonic Knights at the Battle of the Ice
examples: As ruler, Nevsky continued to pay tribute to the Golden Horde, which originated as the northwestern sector of this Asian empire.

clues.clue: Its power began to wane after Dmitri Donskoi's 1380 victory at the Battle of Kulikovo, commemorated in the epic Zadonshchina
examples: This state lost the Battle of Kulikovo to Dimitri Donskoi.
The Zadonshchina recounts a defeat of this polity, earlier centralized by a unification of "Blue" and "White" branches by Tokhtamysh.

clues.clue: Decisively broken when Ivan III forced its retreat at the 1480 Great Stand on the Ugra River
examples: After defeating these people at the Great Stand on the Ugra, Ivan the Great ended their "yoke" of vassalage over Moscow.
A prince earned his nickname "Moneybag" for his loyalty to this state, but his descendant of the same name broke this state's power by forcing them back from the Ugra River in 1480.

works.name: The Ilkhanate
works.description: The Persia-based khanate founded by Hulagu Khan (a Buddhist son of Tolui) south of the Golden Horde, whose subordinate rank -- "il-khan," meaning inferior to the Great Khan -- gives the state its name. Hulagu dismantled the Assassins' Alamut Castle and, in 1258, sacked Baghdad, ending the Abbasid Caliphate; the Tigris allegedly ran black with ink from the destroyed House of Wisdom, and his wife Doquz Khatun, a Christian Khereid princess, persuaded him to spare the city's Christians. Hulagu's forces then lost the 1260 Battle of Ain Jalut to the Mamluks under Baibars, a defeat that spurred failed proposals -- including at the Second Council of Lyons -- for a Franco-Mongol alliance against the Mamluks. Later Ilkhans, such as Ghazan, converted to Islam, and the state's final ruler, Abu Sa'id, may have died of the Black Death.

clues.clue: Founded by Hulagu Khan, a Buddhist son of Tolui, south of the Golden Horde; its name ("il-khan") denotes subordination to the Great Khan
examples: name this Mongol khanate located south of the Golden Horde, which sacked Baghdad under the rule of Hulegu.
name this Persia-based khanate founded by Hulagu Khan, whose name implies that it is inferior to the Mongol Khaganate.

clues.clue: Hulagu dismantled Alamut Castle, the stronghold of the Assassins
examples: One of this title's holders ordered the destruction of Alamut, the base of the Assassins.
The founder of this state dismantled Alamut Castle after the surrender of the Assassins.

clues.clue: Sacked Baghdad in 1258, ending the Abbasid Caliphate; the Tigris allegedly ran black with ink from the destroyed House of Wisdom
examples: A ruler with this title supposedly turned a river black with ink when he sacked Baghdad in 1258.
A nearby river ran black with ink after this event as a result of the destruction of the House of Wisdom.

clues.clue: Hulagu's wife, the Christian Khereid princess Doquz Khatun, persuaded him to spare Baghdad's Christians
examples: Khereid princess Doquz Khatun convinced her husband Hulagu Khan to spare the Christians living in this capital of the Abbasid Caliphate.

clues.clue: Lost the 1260 Battle of Ain Jalut to the Mamluks under Baibars
examples: That founder of this state defeated the Assassins and lost the Battle of Ain Jalut to the Mamluks.
Name this 1260 battle in which Mamluk forces defeated a seemingly unstoppable force from the east that was headed toward Cairo.

clues.clue: Its Ain Jalut defeat spurred failed proposals for a Franco-Mongol alliance against the Mamluks, discussed at the Second Council of Lyons
examples: Plans for achieving this goal drawn up at the Second Council of Lyons fell through due to a lack of royal support.

clues.clue: Later ruler Ghazan converted to Islam; the dynasty's final ruler, Abu Sa'id, may have died of the Black Death
examples: The last ruler of this state, Abu Sa'id, may have died of the Black Death.

works.name: The Dzungars and the Qing Conquest
works.description: The Dzungars were a branch of the Oirat Mongols who built a namesake khanate in present-day Xinjiang; their leader Galdan Khan clashed repeatedly with the Qing Kangxi Emperor, losing the Battle of Jao Modo after his wife Queen Anu died leading a cavalry charge to cover his escape. The first three of the Qianlong Emperor's Ten Great Campaigns targeted the Dzungars in a genocide that, compounded by a smallpox epidemic, killed roughly 80 percent of their population; a later leader, Amursana, led a rebellion crushed in the second of these campaigns, and the Qianlong Emperor's extermination order instructed forces to "show no mercy to these rebels" while sparing only "the old and the weak."

clues.clue: A branch of the Oirat Mongols who ruled a namesake khanate based in present-day Xinjiang
examples: About 40 percent of these people died of smallpox during the Qing conquest of their namesake Khanate based in present-day Xinjiang.
This group now lives mostly in the Xinjiang autonomous region of China.

clues.clue: Leader Galdan Khan's wife, Queen Anu, died leading a cavalry charge to cover his escape from the Kangxi Emperor's forces
examples: In another battle, these people's Queen Anu died while leading a cavalry charge that enabled her husband Galdan to escape an encircling army personally commanded by the Kangxi Emperor.

clues.clue: Galdan Khan was later killed after losing the Battle of Jao Modo to the Kangxi Emperor while trying to conquer the Khalkha Mongols
examples: Another leader of these people was killed after losing the Battle of Jao Modo to the Kangxi Emperor after trying to conquer the Khalkha Mongols.

clues.clue: The first three of the Qianlong Emperor's Ten Great Campaigns targeted them in a genocide that, with smallpox, killed roughly 80 percent of their population
examples: the first three of the Qianlong Emperor's Ten Great Campaigns targeted what branch of the Oirat Mongols in a namesake genocide?
An extermination order by the Qianlong Emperor against these people said to "show no mercy to these rebels" and to "save only the old and the weak".

clues.clue: Leader Amursana's rebellion against Qing forces was crushed in the second of the Ten Great Campaigns
examples: One leader of these people, Amursana, led a rebellion against Qing forces that was crushed in the second of the Ten Great Campaigns.

works.name: Modern Mongolia
works.description: Mongolia declared independence from the collapsing Qing dynasty in 1911, first ruled as the Bogd Khanate; the "Mad Baron" Roman von Ungern-Sternberg briefly restored the Bogd Khan to power in 1921 with a White Russian force before being toppled by the Red Army, after which a Communist government was established and the capital, Niislel Khuree, was renamed Ulaanbaatar ("Red Hero"). As a Soviet satellite whose troops fought the Japanese at the 1939 Battle of Khalkhin Gol, Mongolia endured Stalinist purges under Khorloogiin Choibalsan, the "Stalin of Mongolia," who suppressed Buddhist institutions and hoped -- unsuccessfully -- to reunite Outer and Inner Mongolia. Communist rule ended peacefully in the 1990 Democratic Revolution, centered on Ulaanbaatar's Sukhbaatar Square, and the country has since seen a Buddhist revival and, in 2022, mass protests over the theft of an estimated $13 billion in coal.

clues.clue: Declared independence from the Qing dynasty in 1911, first ruled as the Bogd Khanate
examples: A 1911 revolution led to this country's independence from the Qing dynasty and it was first ruled by the Bogd Khanate.
name this country, the homeland of an empire-builder named Temujin.

clues.clue: The "Mad Baron" Roman von Ungern-Sternberg restored the Bogd Khan to power in 1921 with a White Russian force before being toppled by the Red Army
examples: Bogd Khan was restored to this country's throne by the "Mad Baron" Roman von Ungern-Sternberg.
This country's Communist government was established after expelling an occupying force led by Baron von Ungern-Sternberg that was part of the Russian White Army.

clues.clue: The capital, Niislel Khuree, was renamed Ulaanbaatar ("Red Hero") in 1924
examples: A 1921 communist revolution in this country led to the capital city of Niislel Khuree being given a new name meaning "Red Hero".
Khorloogiin Choibalsan led what country, whose capital was named Ulaanbaatar in 1924?

clues.clue: As a Soviet satellite, its troops fought Japan at the 1939 Battle of Khalkhin Gol
examples: Buryat dissidents were blocked from settling in this country, where the Battle of Khalkhin Gol took place.

clues.clue: Stalinist purges were carried out under Khorloogiin Choibalsan, known as the "Stalin of Mongolia," who suppressed Buddhist institutions
examples: This so-called "Stalin of Mongolia" sought to reunite Outer and Inner Mongolia, but was thwarted by the real Stalin. He served as leader of Mongolia from 1939 to 1952.
Purges in this country led Chairman Khorloogin Choibalsan to be known as this country's Stalin.

clues.clue: Communist rule ended peacefully in the 1990 Democratic Revolution, centered on Ulaanbaatar's Sukhbaatar Square
examples: Protestors against this country's government displayed a flag without the Communist star during 1990 demonstrations in Sukhbaatar Square.
identify this country whose Communist government allowed free elections in 1990.

clues.clue: In 2022, mass protests broke out after an estimated $13 billion in coal was stolen from state reserves
examples: Protests also occurred in Mongolia in 2022 after an estimated $13 billion dollars of this natural resource was stolen.

works.name: Mongol Culture and Religion
works.description: Traditional Mongol religion centers on Tengri, the sky god, alongside a native shamanic tradition that reveres cairn-shrines called ovoo and, in some traditions, a sky goose god presiding over 99 spirits divided into "white" and "black" groups; the fire god Qurmusata King is honored by a taboo against dousing hearth fires. Tibetan Buddhism spread among the Mongols through the Yuan dynasty's ties to Tibet -- the monk Drogon Chogyal Phagpa created the 'Phags-pa script for Kublai Khan -- and later through the Gelug ("Yellow Hat") school; the term "lama," now used broadly in Tibetan Buddhism, originates from the Mongol language. The annual Naadam festival celebrates the "Three Manly Skills" of wrestling, horse racing, and archery, and Mongol musical traditions include overtone throat-singing (closely related to that of neighboring Tuva) and instruments such as the horsehead fiddle (morin khuur).

clues.clue: Traditional shamanism centers on the sky god Tengri and reveres stone-and-wood cairn shrines called ovoo
examples: Many religious sites among these people feature a heap of stone or wood called an ovoo.
Name these peoples who worshiped the creator god Tengri.

clues.clue: The pantheon includes 99 sky spirits divided into "white" and "black" groups, presided over by a sky god in the form of a goose
examples: Their oral tales are known as Uliger, and their pantheon includes a sky god in the form of a goose who presided over 99 similar gods divided into "white" and "black" groups.

clues.clue: The monk Drogon Chogyal Phagpa, at Kublai Khan's request, created the 'Phags-pa script to unite Chinese and Tibetan writing
examples: A missionary to a leader of this ethnic group designed the Phags-pa script.
Drogon Chagyal Phagpa, a member of one of these two ethnic groups, created an eponymous script to assist a dynasty of the other, the Yuan.

clues.clue: The term "lama," now used broadly in Tibetan Buddhism, originates from the Mongol language
examples: These peoples' language is the origin of the term lama used in Tibetan Buddhism, which is common among these people.

clues.clue: The annual Naadam festival features the "Three Manly Skills" of wrestling, horse racing, and archery
examples: The Naadam festival in this country includes competitions in its "Three Manly Skills" of wrestling, horse racing, and archery.

clues.clue: Practice overtone throat-singing (closely related to that of neighboring Tuva) and play the horsehead fiddle, or morin khuur
examples: name these people who, like the neighboring Tuvans, practice overtone throat singing.
Other instruments of this people include a clarinet called the ever buree, the "horsehead fiddle" or morin khuur, and the tovshuur lute.

clues.clue: Traditionally live in portable, felt-covered tent dwellings called gers (yurts)
examples: Identify these portable residences that are erected near good pastureland in parts of Central Asia.

comprehensive_summary: The Mongols are a Central Asian ethnic group of nomadic horsemen unified in 1206 by Temujin, who took the title Genghis Khan; their rise is recounted in the Secret History of the Mongols, discovered in the 19th century by the Archimandrite Palladius, which opens with a mythic genealogy tracing the Mongols to a blue-gray wolf and a red doe and an ancestress, Alan Gua, impregnated by a "glittering spirit," and which narrates the kidnapping and rescue of Temujin's wife Borte, his blood-brotherhood (anda) with and later break from Jamukha, and his creation of the elite kheshig bodyguard, the yam relay-messenger system, and a secret, largely religion-tolerant law code called the Yassa. New khans were subsequently elected at tribal councils called kurultai. After Genghis Khan's death, his empire expanded and then split among his descendants. In China, the Mongols under Ogedei toppled the Jin dynasty and, after the Toluid Civil War delayed the campaign, Kublai Khan finished off the Southern Song at the 1279 Battle of Yamen, founding the Yuan Dynasty, which ruled from Shangdu and Khanbaliq, issued chao paper currency, commissioned the 'Phags-pa script, hosted Marco Polo and Odoric of Pordenone, and fell in 1368 to Zhu Yuanzhang's Red Turban Rebellion. Kublai Khan's two invasions of Japan (1274 and 1281) both failed after being destroyed by kamikaze typhoons, a defeat memorialized in Takezaki Suenaga's battle scroll and one that weakened Japan's Kamakura shogunate. In Europe, Batu Khan's 1241 invasion, masterminded by the general Subotai, killed Duke Henry the Pious at the Battle of Legnica and annihilated Hungarian forces at the Battle of Mohi, only ending when Great Khan Ogedei's death forced a retreat east. Batu Khan then founded the Golden Horde, which ruled Russia from Sarai and imposed tributary "yoke" status via yarlyk patents -- even Alexander Nevsky paid tribute -- until Dmitri Donskoi's 1380 victory at Kulikovo and Ivan III's 1480 Great Stand on the Ugra River broke its power. In Persia, Hulagu Khan founded the Ilkhanate, dismantling the Assassins' Alamut fortress and sacking Baghdad in 1258 (with his Christian wife Doquz Khatun sparing the city's Christians) before losing the 1260 Battle of Ain Jalut to the Mamluks, a defeat that spurred failed Franco-Mongol alliance talks against the Mamluks; later Ilkhans like Ghazan converted to Islam before the state collapsed. A remnant Oirat Mongol group, the Dzungars, built a khanate in Xinjiang under leaders like Galdan Khan before being destroyed in a genocide during the first three of the Qing Qianlong Emperor's Ten Great Campaigns. Mongolia itself declared independence from the Qing in 1911 as the Bogd Khanate, was briefly restored under the "Mad Baron" Ungern-Sternberg, then became a Soviet-aligned Communist state renaming its capital Ulaanbaatar, enduring Stalinist purges under Khorloogiin Choibalsan before its peaceful 1990 Democratic Revolution. Throughout, Mongol religious and cultural life centered on sky-god Tengri worship and shamanic ovoo shrines, later blended with Tibetan Buddhism via the 'Phags-pa script and Gelug "Yellow Hat" lamas, alongside the Naadam festival's "Three Manly Skills," overtone throat-singing, and the traditional felt ger dwelling.
